text: Intel Posts Strongest Revenue Growth in More Than 15 Years, with 2Q26 Revenue Reaching $16.1B  Intel reported 2Q26 revenue of $16.1B on July 23, representing 25% YoY growth and once again exceeding guidance. CEO Lip-Bu Tan described the quarter as Intel's strongest revenue growth in more than 15 years, highlighting that AI is driving unprecedented demand for computing. He added that Intel is well positioned to sustain long-term growth through its CPU portfolio, AI ASICs, advanced packaging technologies, and expanding foundry ecosystem.   hashtag #AIInferenceDemandDrives59PercentGrowthinDCAI Intel's Data Center and AI (DCAI) business generated $6.3B in revenue during the quarter, up 59% YoY, making it the company's fastest-growing business segment. Meanwhile, the Client Computing and Physical AI Group (CCPG) reported revenue of $8.9B, an increase of 13% YoY. The company partnered with SambaNova and Foxconn to showcase a production-ready, rack-scale infrastructure platform for Agentic AI inference. Additionally, Vector Core Compute (VC2) introduced a distributed agentic AI cloud solution integrating Intel Xeon processors, SambaNova RDUs, and NVIDIA Blackwell GPUs.   hashtag #18APEntersRiskProductionasIntelExpandsCapacity Intel Foundry reported $5.8B in revenue, up 31% YoY. On the manufacturing front, Intel 18A-P entered risk production on schedule, delivering further improvements in performance, power efficiency, and thermal characteristics. Selected Core Ultra Series 3 (Panther Lake) processors have also entered volume production using ASML's High-NA EUV (EXE) lithography platform. Intel announced a €5B investment to expand manufacturing capacity, increasing output of Intel 3-based Xeon 6 and next-generation Xeon processors. The company further expanded its custom silicon portfolio through a partnership with Fortinet to develop security processors, broadening its ASIC business beyond networking and IPUs.   hashtag #3Q26RevenueGuidedtoUS16Point8B For 3Q26, Intel expects revenue of $15.8–16.8B. CFO David Zinsner said stronger-than-expected shipments were driven by higher manufacturing yields and shorter production cycles. As AI computing demand continues to accelerate, Intel plans to significantly increase investments in manufacturing equipment, cleanroom capacity, and advanced substrates to support growth across both its product portfolio and foundry business over the next two years. Operating cash flow reached $7B during the quarter.  📊 More semiconductor insights: https://buff.ly/UWCDvd1 📊 More industry charts: https://buff.ly/4hbxzLr

As test-time scaling takes hold, the focus of the AI industry is quickly shifting from training to inference.  Behind this shift lies a structural change in the memory hierarchy. HBM defined the training era. But inference requires more, giving rise to HBF, SSD POD, and SRAM to push past the limits of capacity and bandwidth. 【Aggressive Module House Stocking Supports NAND Spot Prices, but Rebound Momentum Remains Weak】  Overall spot market remains sluggish due to withering consumer demand. However, a certain module house, who recently saw a high visibility on enterprise SSD orders, has turned to the spot market to stock up aggressively, as suppliers are insufficient in wafers and higher in quotations. This led to a temporal halt to the depletion of spot prices. Having said that, most buyers have reached their limit of cost tolerance, aside from stocking activities among specific module houses, where order pulls from the consumer end remains on the passive end. Without support from substantial buy orders, the overall spot market still lacks momentum in price rebounds. Spot prices of 512Gb TLC wafers dropped by 1.28% this week (July 20), arriving at US$18.931.  📷 Related news from  hashtag #TrendForce: https://buff.ly/3xtxMXH 🔗 📷 For more on  hashtag #NAND Price: https://buff.ly/3VLrT2w 🔗

【DRAM Spot Market Edges Higher as Branded DDR4 Demand Supports Prices】  The DRAM spot market has seen a slight overall uptick this week, with inquiries largely focused on branded DDR4 chips. However, transactions are generally limited due to suppliers’ reluctance to sell at perceived low prices and buyers’ hesitation to chase higher prices. The average spot price of mainstream chips (i.e., DDR4 1Gx8 3200MT/s) increased by 3.27% from US$39.8 last week (July 15) to US$41.1 this week (July 21).  📷 Related news: https://buff.ly/3U1g2fb 🔗 📷 For more on  hashtag #DRAM Price: https://buff.ly/3VK49vr 🔗

TSMC Delivers Strong 2Q26 Results as 2nm Process Contributes Revenue for the First Time  TSMC reported strong results for 2Q26, driven by robust demand from the HPC market. Quarterly revenue reached $40.2B, up 33.7% YoY, while gross margin climbed to 67.7%. Net income surged 77.4% YoY, setting a new company record.  Advanced Nodes Approach 80% of Revenue TSMC announced that its 2nm process contributed wafer revenue for the first time in 2Q26, accounting for 3% of total wafer revenue. Combined with 3nm (30%), 5nm (33%), and 7nm (11%), technologies at 7nm and below represented 77% of total wafer revenue during the quarter.  The company also noted that, as 2nm remains in the capacity ramp-up phase, inventory days increased by seven days QoQ to 87 days.  Cost Optimization Offsets Overseas Expansion Pressure TSMC attributed its 1.5 percentage-point QoQ improvement in gross margin to 67.7% primarily to internal cost optimization and higher capacity utilization. These gains were partially offset by the margin dilution associated with the ongoing expansion of overseas fabs. Capital expenditures totaled $15.7B during the quarter.  North America Remains TSMCs Largest Market From an end-market perspective, HPC remained the primary growth driver, contributing 66% of quarterly revenue and growing 20% QoQ. Geographically, customers in North America accounted for 78% of total revenue, maintaining the region's position as TSMC's largest market.  📊 More semiconductor insights: https://buff.ly/3LTbtDT 📊 More TSMC financial charts: https://buff.ly/4cYZj3N
